content.problemStatement:
RJ is a very curious observer. On the first day of every month, he tries to figure out, for each of the seven days of the week, how many times that day occurs in the current month.

RJ got confused so badly doing this that he asks for your help!
He asks several queries; in each query, he gives you the number of days in the current month and which day of the week is on the 1st of the current month.

For each query, you should tell him how many times each day of the week occurs.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains an integer W (28 ≤ W ≤ 31) and a string S denoting the weekday of the 1st day of the month. S is one of: mon, tues, wed, thurs, fri, sat, sun.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line with seven space‑separated integers: the occurrences of Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday and Sunday in that month.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First month has 28 days → exactly 4 of each weekday. Second month has 31 days, starts on Wednesday, so Wednesday, Thursday and Friday get an extra occurrence.
30 days → base 4 each, remainder 2. Starting day is Sunday, so Sunday and Monday receive the extra day.
29 days → base 4, remainder 1. The extra day falls on Thursday.

solution.approach: Each month has at least floor(W/7) occurrences of every weekday. Let base = W / 7 (integer division) and r = W % 7. The first r days starting from the given first‑day receive one extra occurrence. Map the weekday string to an index 0..6 (mon=0,…,sun=6), fill an array cnt[7] with base, then for i from 0 to r‑1 increment cnt[(startIdx+i)%7]. Finally print cnt in order Monday to Sunday.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a small fixed‑size array is used.
